items.title: Linear regression as a smooth bowl
items.scenario: You fit house prices with one feature and MSE loss. With parameters (slope w, intercept b), the MSE loss over (w, b) is a convex parabolic bowl—any downhill path from a reasonable start reaches the same global minimum.
items.explanation: This is the beginner-friendly loss surface: one valley, no misleading local minima. It anchors what 'nice' optimization geometry looks like before neural nets.

items.title: Neural net with two weights visualized
items.scenario: A tiny two-weight network on XOR is trained while logging loss on a grid of (w₁, w₂) values. The contour plot shows multiple basins and saddle-like regions; gradient descent from different initializations lands in different valleys with different test accuracy.
items.explanation: Even in 2D parameter slices, non-convex structure appears. The surface picture explains why initialization and optimizer matter beyond the loss formula alone.

items.title: Plateau after a learning-rate drop
items.scenario: A vision model's training loss curve flattens for many epochs after you halve the learning rate. Inspecting a 2D slice near the current weights shows a wide flat valley floor—gradients are small in every direction, so each step barely changes loss.
items.explanation: Plateaus are a landscape feature, not necessarily a bug. Recognizing flat regions helps you decide whether to increase learning rate, add momentum, or change parameterization rather than blindly training longer.

items.title: Sharp vs flat minimum and test error
items.scenario: Two runs reach similar training loss on CIFAR-10 but different test accuracy. Perturbing weights by small random noise hurts one model far more than the other—the sharper valley overfits memorized patterns; the flatter valley generalizes better.
items.explanation: Loss surface geometry connects to generalization: where you land on the landscape (valley width/curvature) can matter as much as the training loss value at the bottom.
